My life looked like that at one point. I hit rock bottom and I felt like a failure every day. I gave in to every cheap dopamine possible, but after a few months of struggling to climb back up, I finally had control of my life again. If you're also going through life like him, I want to show you how you can get out of your rut and finally take back control of your life again. Step one, clean up yourself. When our life begins to fall apart, so does our environment. Messy room, unclean dishes, dirty clothes, the space we occupy is usually a reflection of ourselves. If your room is disordered and looks like a mess, it usually means that your current life is also a mess right now. If you fail to take care of your space, it means you fail to take care of yourself as well. So, your breath starts to stink and your body starts to reek of the smell of rotten fish. If you can't take care of yourself on the most basic level, how do you expect to actually take back control of your life? So, get up and start to clean your space and you'll actually feel like you're slowly building your life back together. Then, go and improve on your hygiene. Brush your teeth, have a nice shower, put on deodorant, smell good and feel good. Treat yourself with respect. Being clean and feeling fresh can boost your self-esteem over time. You're basically telling yourself that you're someone worth taking care of. Step two, figure out who you want to become. There's a reason why most of us choose to use the same four apps for hours while rotting in bed all day. It's not because we're lazy, it's because we don't have a mission or a purpose to work towards that pushes us out of bed every morning. When we lack a goal or direction in life, we allow ourselves to be pushed around by life hoping to get lucky and end up somewhere we want, [music] but usually we just end back in a rut. To have a sense of direction in life, you need two things. First, figure out the person you ultimately want to become and the person you want to be proud of. What does the person look like? The things they do don't do on a daily basis, the type of life they have, the things [music] they tell themselves. Write this person down on a piece of paper in as much detail as possible. We want to create a new identity for ourselves because your current identity right now and the things you tell yourself will only hold you back from improving. Second, write down two to three personal goals that could be realistically achieved in one to two years. In the past, I've tried writing multiple different goals that covered all areas of my life. But these goals always faded away because I was getting lost in the sheer amount of goals I set for myself and it was just impossible to put all of my attention to all of them. But when I just stuck to two to three goals, it became a lot easier to prioritize and I had a clearer sense of direction. [music] When you have these two written down, stick it on your wall so you are constantly reminded of the transformational journey you are on. Step three, do the next small habit. Once you have the ideal person you want to be and the two to three goals you want to achieve, it's time to finally take action. You need to ask yourself, "What is the next smallest habit you can build that will move you closer to your goals and the person you want to be?" The reason we want to take small steps is because we want this process to be as easy and as painless as possible. Most people fail to take action because all they think about is the long and grueling journey up ahead. They try to eliminate all the bad habits at once and flood their days with high productive tasks, but they just end up getting burnt out and giving up. Trust me, I've been there before. Instead, just focus on the next one to two small habits you need to build. For example, if you want to become a more productive person, try setting an alarm and waking up just 30 minutes earlier than usual and also not looking at your phone for the first 30 minutes of your day. Once those two become a habit, figure out the next habit you need to build in order to get closer to your identity and your goal. Then rinse and repeat. Step four, structure your day. When your days are not structured and you don't have a schedule to follow, you'll be stuck wondering what you should do at every minute, constantly making decisions on whether you should go do something productive or go binge on Netflix. But, when your day is structured, and when you have a schedule to follow, it's a lot easier to stay on track, and you have more control over your life, cuz you'll know what you should be doing at each exact moment. At first, you don't have to go military-style and plan a crazy strict schedule. [music] For now, just start by writing down three tasks you want to complete each day that move you closer to your goals. Write it somewhere where you can physically see and check it off, because the act of finishing and checking off a task give you a sense of accomplishment and helps you feel like you've won the day. And it doesn't have matter how big or small these tasks are, as long as they are pushing you towards the self you are trying to become. Once you get the ball rolling more, you can then start actually creating a schedule for yourself. Step five, move your body. Wouldn't you agree that life would be better if we all had a magical pill that could improve your mental clarity, prolong your lifespan, improve your mental and physical energy, increase your overall mood and outlook on life, improve your sleep, and increase your physical attractiveness? Well, that pill is actually real, and it's called [music] exercise. Our human body is built for movement, not sitting on the couch [music] all day. Just like brushing your teeth, exercise should be a non-negotiable part of your daily routine. So, pick up those dumbbells, lace up your sneakers, and get your heart pumping. Embrace the movement your body was made for. Step six, do what you said you would do. As you're settling into the schedule you made for yourself and start making some progress, >> [music] >> the task you set yourself to do won't become easy. In fact, they might just become more challenging and more repetitive. There will be times where you don't feel like doing the hard [music] work or putting in the effort. This is where many people get stuck, eventually losing their momentum and slowly slipping back into their old habits. To overcome this, you have to [music] be disciplined and commit to completing the task you set for yourself, even when you don't feel like doing them. How much [music] you put into this world is how much you will get out of it. By completing the task you said you would do, you build trust within yourself. As you trust yourself more, your confidence in yourself grows alongside, making you more likely to reach your goals and less likely to give up. Step seven, consistency. There will be times when your past bad habits creep back in, and some days you will fall off track. Know that your journey won't be linear, [music] but as long as you keep moving forward and stay consistent, you will always have a chance of success till the day you die. it's surprisingly cheap. I'll get into the actual

about 30% of the total hospital bill and sometimes it can
be reduced down to 10%, depending on other reasons. And your monthly health insurance
varies depending on your age, income, type of work
and where you live. There are three main
health care systems in Japan: one for company employees, two for civil servants,
teachers and public workers, and the third national
insurance is for everyone else. They all have pretty much

goes to the hospital so many times. Here are some regular
doctor visits that I've had. So my knee was bothering me
after playing some basketball I went to the doctor for

to consult with the doctor. Can you guys see now why
Maiko goes to the doctor so much? It's just so cheap,

it makes so much more sense to me why Japanese people go
to the hospital and go to the clinic for every little sickness,

company health insurance is that they offer free
annual health checkups. It's funny though,
because health examinations become a yearly talking point

if they've taken it yet, regardless, you know, it's that time of the year,

hearing, blood, chest x-rays, urinalysis, etc. Just to make sure your

more detailed bloodwork, etc. Then you can add extra examinations

medical expense supply, which basically means that

A third of students in South Korea have contemplated ending their lives because of academic pressure. In Japan, self-inflicted death is the leading cause of death for children. In China, self-inflicted death is so common that schools install metal bars on windows to prevent students from jumping off. Why are so many students in Asia so depressed? Because the infrastructure of many Asian countries rely on an education system comparable to modern slavery. And you'll soon see what I mean. These images look straight out of a dystopian movie, but this is actually the college entrance exam that every Chinese teenager must take, known as the G call. A low score on this exam could mean a lowinccome job and discrimination from society. The G call is treated like a national holiday. All construction stops. Traffic diversions are created. Airplane paths are changed to reduce noise. The exam room is full of cameras, metal detectors, and fingerprint and iris matching technology. Cheating on this exam can lead to 7 years in prison. And other Asian countries operate with a very similar system. Japan has the common test. India has the J. South Korea has the Sunyong. These exams are packed with extremely difficult questions which require years of intense studying to pass. They determine the future of millions of students and the mental health effects can be seen in the thousands who end their lives every year. But what exactly do these students go through to make life a living hell? If you imagine what slavery is like, you might imagine 16 hours of work a day, a lack of freedom, and physical abuse from authorities. Well, that's exactly what a student in Asia goes through. Do you ever find school exhausting? Well, get ready to change your mindset because here's the study schedule of a South Korean student. Wake up at 6:30 in the morning. Get to school at 8:00 a.m. School ends at 4:00 p.m. And that's probably when you would go home and doom scroll. But not these South Korean students. They have to attend a cram school. Creme schools are private tutoring institutions that put students through long study sessions in high pressure environments. A student gets out of cramm school around 9:00 p.m. But they aren't done yet. They then go back to their school library and self-study until 11:00 p.m. Wow, that sounds like a long rough day, huh? Well, it's not over yet. They then go back home and continue studying until 2:00 a.m. That only leaves them with 4 hours to sleep before they wake up and do it all again. Why do they study so hard? Because if they don't do well on their entrance exam, they won't attend a good university, and they'll be stuck with a low-income job and a difficult life. Their intense study schedule isn't an option, it's a necessity. And it's not just South Korea. A 16-hour study schedule is very common for students all across Asia. And along with this, there's also a very toxic culture around competition at Asian schools. All grades and test scores are publicly displayed for parents and students to see. And having a low score could mean shame, insecurity, and even bullying. This creates additional pressure to study harder. And if that isn't bad enough, physical abuse is another factor contributing to high self-inflicted death rates in Asia. Imagine you're in high school and you hand your homework to the teacher past the due date. The teacher stands up and slaps you in the face. You'd probably be in shock, but to a student in Asia, it's just part of life. The reason I know this is because I've seen it myself. When I was 6 years old, I went to China for vacation. For whatever reason, my parents put me in an elementary school there for a few days. When I was there, I witnessed a very strange method of teaching. Teachers would call on a student to answer a math question. If that student gave the wrong answer, the student got smacked with a wooden stick. And remember, these are six-year-olds that I'm talking about. Corporal punishment is illegal in many Asian countries, but that doesn't prevent teachers from doing it. Reporting this act is an extremely complicated process. So as a result, teachers almost always get away with it. In some cases, parents even prefer teachers who implement physical punishment. There are countless anecdotes and video evidence on the internet of students in Asia being hit by their teachers. There have even been some cases where students were beaten to death. In Taiwan, a whopping 70% of junior high students had experienced physical abuse from their teachers. In 2011, 98% of South Korean students reported physical punishment from their teachers. One school in China even forced parents to consent to their children being hit. This tradition of abuse has been passed on throughout centuries. And because it's so culturally accepted, Asia just turns a blind eye to it. Several self-inflicted deaths in Asia have been directly attributed to physical punishment from teachers. And studies have proven a direct link between physical punishment and the desire for self-inflicted death. And if you think this is bad enough, let me remind you that in Asia, talking about your mental health is like talking about your diarrhea. It's just not something you talk about. This is because Asian culture is centered around perfection. And any issue of mental health is a sign of weakness. Valuing mental health is seen as shameful and selfish, as if a person only cares about themselves and not the people around them. As a result, Asian students hide their emotions and struggle, and they almost never seek help. 70% of South Korean students who ended their lives did not show any unusual characteristics at all. This also means that essential mental health resources such as therapy, psychiatry, and counseling are very limited and students rarely use them. Mental illness is rarely diagnosed or treated in Asia and many Asian people even deny the existence of disorders like depression or ADHD because they see it as a weakness. Now, combine everything I've just discussed with frequent bullying between students and abuse from parents, and it should be no surprise that so many students wish to end their lives. However, what remains shocking is that children under 14 are doing the exact same thing. In recent years, South Korea saw an increase in self-inflicted death in girls between 10 and 14 years old, and China saw a similar increase in children between 5 and 14 years old. These children should be playing with toys and watching Spongebob. But in Asia, children study up to 11 hours a day. One reason for this is because class grades play a crucial role in college acceptance. Another reason is that many Asian countries also adopt an exam system for entering junior high and high school. A bad score on these exams could mean losing the opportunity for a proper education or being forced into vocational school, which prepares students for labor work. As a result, the pressure to do well is extremely high in children as well. This video of a little girl begging her parents for more playtime says it all. [Music] With death rates so high, you might be wondering how Asian governments are responding to this problem. Well, there have been some attempts to lessen academic pressure, but it's not nearly enough. Many Asian governments have cracked down on cram schools to reduce student workload. South Korea is planning to ban cram schools from operating past 10 p.m. Many Asian countries have set limits on how much homework a student can receive. Countries have also implemented crisis hotlines and are attempting to provide students with more mental health resources. But these attempts don't change the fact that college admissions are still extremely competitive and many parents and students are secretly continuing intense studying and lessons. The pressure to do well remains extremely high. Unless Asian countries reform their education systems, nothing will get better. Which begs the question, what changes do Asian countries really need? Well, an entrance exam shouldn't be the sole determining factor for college acceptance. People are more than just test scores. There are multiple categories of intelligence, and everyone has their own individual strengths and weaknesses. But Asian education systems do not recognize this. Their worth to society is not in their unique traits, but rather a numerical value on their exam. Compare this to Western countries where college acceptance isn't just determined by test scores, but also essays and personal statements, letters of recommendation, and extracurricular activities in which a student's individuality shines through. Exam scores only measure how much information a student can memorize. These exams do not measure a student's creativity or intelligence or real world problem solving abilities which are qualities that should matter the most to a society. Through this system, many bright and capable students are losing the opportunity to change the world. Asia's education system reveals a very dark future that Asia is hitting down. Although Asian governments and parents have gradually realized this problem, more action needs to be taken. As populations rise and job markets shrink, competition will rise drastically and education requirements will only increase, students will continue to suffer in a demanding abusive system. If creativity is not valued in Asian nations, then people will stop being creative. But this decline of creativity is actually something happening all around the world and the results will soon be devastating. If you want to know what I'm talking about, then click right here. As you can see, I'm a very small channel. So, hey guys, please please subscribe, please like, you know, I'd really appreciate that. I have a lot more videos coming soon, so I'll see you guys later.

Burnout.  It was one of the most difficult
periods of my life.  I got severely ill.  I had to stop my residency.  I had to quit something I had been

In the early 1990s, the most profitable game in the world wasn't called Mario or Sonic. It was Street Fighter >> [music] >> and yet this game was born from a chain of accidents, a series of unbelievable coincidences, burned-out developers, bugs that somehow made it into the final build, a component shortage that reshuffled everything, and a creator who stumbled into the video game industry purely by chance while trying to become a journalist. All of this happened at a time when arcades were dying. This is the story of Street Fighter II, a game with a near miraculous development that would go on to [music] conquer the world and change the history of video games forever. >> [music] [music] >> In the early 1980s, a young Japanese man named Takashi Nishiyama had just finished his communication studies and wanted to become a journalist. But he struggled to find work. At the same time, the video game market was booming in Japan driven by the massive success of Space Invaders. Game centers were popping up everywhere and after playing these games, Nishiyama found the experience fascinating. He soon got hired at a new company called Irem, not far from where he lived. There he created his first games, some of which were well received like the shoot 'em up Moon Patrol. But more importantly, he created a true arcade pillar with Kung Fu Master, one of the pioneers of what would later be called beat 'em ups. The game was even adapted to the Nintendo Entertainment System by Shigeru Miyamoto and his team, who reused some of its ideas to create Super Mario Brothers, like having bosses at the end of levels. Nishiyama designed the game based on his passion for martial arts, but especially on film starring Bruce Lee. It was directly inspired by the movie Game of Death, where Bruce Lee climbs a building floor by floor, facing different opponents on each level, and by Jackie Chan's film Wheels on Meals, from which it even borrowed the Japanese title Spartan X. During the game's development and before its release, one of Nishiyama's colleagues, Kenzo Tsujimoto, left Irem and founded a new company called Capcom, originally named Capsule Computer, which initially supplied electronic hardware for arcade machines. After pushing hard, Tsujimoto managed to recruit Nishiyama, whom he considered an excellent game designer. After creating a few arcade games with moderate success, including Trojan, a beat 'em up that featured a versus mode, something very rare at the time, Nishiyama visited arcades and noticed the same pattern everywhere, shoot 'em ups, racing games, and a few beat 'em ups. He came to the conclusion that arcade games were starting to feel repetitive. So, during a meeting at Capcom, he pitched a completely new idea, a massive flashy arcade cabinet that would capture the feeling of martial arts. Management was hesitant. It would be very expensive, but at the same time, Capcom had just realized that Sega and Namco, the arcade giants of the time, made most of their money by selling large arcade machines, so they gave the green light. Nishiyama got to work and brought in Hiroshi Matsumoto, who also had a passion for martial arts, as a designer on the project, without having any idea of the nightmare development that awaited them. At first, they sat down and thought about how to capture the essence of martial arts in an arcade machine, and they came up with several ideas. It would be a one-on-one duel, either against the computer or another player. What they loved about martial arts was the wide variety of moves depending on the discipline. And Nishiyama absolutely wanted the machines require physical effort from the player, so it would truly feel like martial arts. Inspired by electronic machines where players had to break bricks with their bare hands. The wide variety of moves led to the idea of having many different characters and confronting different fighting styles. That's how 12 characters were created, which was huge for the time. There were Ryu and Ken masters, martial arts masters trained by the same teacher. One Japanese and the other American using a mix of judo and karate. There was Geki, a ninja. Retsu, a fallen monk. Mike, a boxer. Joe, an American kickboxer. Lee, a Chinese kung fu master. And Birdie, a British street brawler. Nishiyama and Matsumoto studied many techniques from these martial arts and assigned them to the characters, exaggerating them to make them more impactful. Some were more original like Ryu's energy projectile, the Hadouken, inspired by the Hadouho, a special attack from the spaceship in Space Battleship Yamato. The game gradually took shape as a series of fights across five different countries with distinct stages for each. Nishiyama decided to frame it as an ultimate world tournament to justify these confrontations. And he also created detailed profiles for each character, describing everything from their favorite food to their motivation for entering the tournament. All of this was designed to make the characters feel more real and therefore more engaging. And for the third goal, making the player feel the same sensations as a real martial arts fight. Hiroshi Matsumoto and Takashi Nishiyama came up with the pneumatic buttons that could measure how hard you hit them. The harder you pressed, the stronger your character's attack would be. These inputs also had to be combined with precise joystick motions to perform special moves, what we now call quarter circle inputs, which seem to have been designed specifically to prevent players from pulling them off by just mashing buttons. They also helped sync the player's input with the character's animation and made up for the lack of buttons since the cabinet only had two large pneumatic pads. But Capcom was still a small studio at the time, just starting to build its own arcade cabinets and didn't have the experience to design something like this. So they reached out to Atari in the United States and asked them to handle the pneumatic system and cabinet design, a process that ended up taking several months. And that's when the nightmare began. While Atari worked on the cabinet, the development team had to keep building the game. But to test the controls, they only had access to an unprotected prototype of those pneumatic buttons. After repeated testing, they ended up literally smashing their hands against the machine over and over again, adjusting and retesting each time until their hands were swollen. And it got worse. The person in charge of programming the cabinet wasn't even a real programmer. He was a systems engineer put on the project simply because no one else was available and he didn't really understand programming. As a result, Matsumoto had to handle the character programming and animations himself frame by frame, which was incredibly slow and exhausting. >> [music] >> I mentioned this in my video on id Software for Doom. The team used a wax model and photographed it from multiple angles to create animations. Because on Wolfenstein 3D, they had to animate everything by hand frame by frame, which they described as torture. [music] But here, it was even worse. There were far more characters and animations to create. It was a nightmare. Nishiyama and Matsumoto pulled all-nighters, pushing themselves on the brink of exhaustion. The project fell far behind schedule, and several parts of the game had to be cut. As Nishiyama later recalled, "We really wanted to make all the characters playable, and we had a lot of other ideas to improve the game. But unfortunately, between budget cuts and the major delays, we could only make Ryu and Ken playable." Nishiyama would later describe the finished game as only about 70% of his original vision, disappointed that he couldn't take it further. The cabinet designed by Atari was approved. It was massive, [music] featuring a 26-in screen, about 40% larger than standard arcade displays, two large pressure-sensitive buttons, and enough space for one or two players. In August 1987, the arcade machine was finalized under the name Street Fighter. It was tested across several regions, and the player response was quite good. But what stood out was that the pressure-based controls were simply too exhausting. Players liked the machine, but didn't have the stamina to play for long. And more importantly, Capcom learned that some players had actually injured themselves using it. Fearing potential lawsuits, they forced Nishiyama to change the controls, something he saw as a personal failure. So, Nishiyama went back to the drawing board and came up with a new control scheme, an eight-direction joystick paired with six buttons. Capcom wasn't on board. They just wanted a simple setup with two buttons and a joystick to keep things accessible. But Nishiyama argued that six buttons were actually more intuitive, three for kicks, three for punches, each with light, medium, and heavy variations. It was rejected at first, but he fought hard against the marketing department who didn't want it, and eventually got his way. Very quickly, this simpler and cheaper version replaced the original, and as soon as it did, the game became a notable success. Arcade operators even ranked it as the most popular arcade cabinet in Japan in January 1988, far from the failure it is sometimes portrayed as. It also reached the top 10 in the United States and hit number one in the UK in August 1988, according to Coin Slot magazine. The Street Fighter franchise was born. The game was ported to many different systems, [music] including the ZX Spectrum, Commodore 64, MS-DOS, Amiga, and PC Engine under the name Fighting Street. Players loved the characters, each with detailed backstories, something that really set the game apart. They loved [music] that special moves weren't documented. You had to discover them by playing, which led to all kinds of rumors and urban legends. They loved being able to face off against friends, the challenge of certain fights, the depth of the move set, and the unpredictable patterns of the AI. A niche group of players fell in love with the game and the emotions it delivered, something they couldn't find anywhere else. Unfortunately, the success didn't last. After bringing in some money for Capcom, the game gradually disappeared from arcades both in Japan and the US. But what no one could have imagined at the time was that Street Fighter was about to rise from the dead and take over the world, all thanks to an unexpected bug. A few weeks after the release of Street Fighter in arcades, Capcom is hit by a major shakeup. Takashi Nishiyama leaves the company, taking part of his team with him, one of Capcom's core development groups. Nishiyama didn't get along with his superiors, and he had grown tired of the lack of creative freedom, as well as the tight timeline and budget constraints that prevented Street Fighter from being as deep as he wanted. Meanwhile, another company offered him an extremely attractive deal with far greater creative freedom. The company called SNK. At the time, SNK and Capcom were essentially at war. A few years earlier, Capcom had poached staff from SNK using controversial methods, which reportedly enraged SNK's president, sometimes nicknamed the terrifying boxer by employees. So, bringing Nishiyama and part of his team over to SNK also served as a form of retaliation for what Capcom had done years prior. This departure is a major blow for Capcom. Street Fighter had been a notable success, and they wanted to produce a sequel, but much of the original team was now gone. Capcom USA pushes for it, insisting the game has strong commercial potential. Capcom Japan eventually agrees and assigns the project to Yoshiki Okamoto, known for producing 1942 and Ghouls 'n Ghosts. He brings in Akira Nishitani and Akira Yasuda, better known as Akiman, two developers considered somewhat eccentric within Capcom. But at that time, a shortage of electronic components is affecting the video game industry. Using this as an excuse, Nishitani and Akiman deliberately steer away from making a true sequel since they weren't that interested in Street Fighter. Instead, inspired by the massive arcade success of Double Dragon, they develop a beat-'em-up, which they present at trade shows under the name Street Fighter 1989. However, attendees quickly point out that it has nothing in common with the original Street Fighter. The team had assumed the first game had been forgotten, but that wasn't the case. [music] At the same time, Capcom begins receiving letters from fans of the original game, letters that came from many different countries. Fearing disappointment, they ultimately rename the project Final Fight. Released in 1989, it becomes a massive hit, one of Capcom's biggest arcade successes. But even after Final Fight, players are still asking for a true sequel to Street Fighter. Capcom USA once again pressures Capcom Japan, and this time the project is clearly defined from the start, Street Fighter II. Okamoto steps in more firmly, closely overseeing Nishitani and Akiman to ensure they actually deliver a real sequel. But by then, conditions have changed dramatically. Thanks to the success of Final Fight, Capcom now has far more resources to allocate to the project. In a twist of fate, without Final Fight, this sequel would likely never have become what it did. Nishitani and Akiman launch into an unusually ambitious development. At a time when most game teams consist of seven or eight people, nearly 40 developers end up working on Street Fighter As associate producer, Noritaka Funamizu recalls, "Akiman and Nishitani were in charge of character design and game design, two undeniably crazy individuals. They kept expanding the project until it became massive. It was the biggest development we had ever done at the time, with around 20 designers just working on characters. It was enormous, and since the project took much longer than expected, people were saying that if Street Fighter II failed, Capcom would go under." Okamoto agrees to provide the resources, but Nishitani and Akiman don't fully grasp the stakes. When designing characters and moves, their main goal is simply to create things they find fun. That's how they come up with Dhalsim, an Indian fighter inspired by fakirs and Hindu yogis, pushing the stereotype to the extreme with yoga poses and stretchy limbs. They continue creating characters one after another, inspired by Harmagedon, Genma Wars. They design a Chinese female fighter who then evolves into a kung fu expert, Chun-Li. Then come an American Air Force stereotype from the 1980s, who becomes Guile, a man raised by animals named Annabi Bi, who eventually turns into the Brazilian jungle beast Blanka, a Soviet Greco-Roman wrestler, initially named Vodka Gorbachevsky, later renamed Zangief, and a sumo wrestler representing Japan, first simply called Sumo before becoming Honda. They also bring back characters from the original game, such as Ken and Sagat, bringing the roster to seven playable figures and four bosses. In the design documents, two elements remain, a ninja in the abilities column, and Spain in the countries column. So, amused by the idea, they combine the two and create a Spanish ninja. When Akiman checks back in with the character designers, he pushes them to go even further with clichés and stereotypes, to hold nothing back. So, they followed his lead. Blanka turns bright green, Honda adopts the ceremonial makeup and attire of sumo wrestlers, and one character evolves into a boxer inspired by Mike Tyson. Akiman wants every character to be instantly recognizable, distinct, and so exaggerated that they leave a strong impression. But, more importantly, he wants each of them to have their own unique gameplay. That's how Zangief, the wrestler, is given a wide variety of grappling moves. Honda charges forward with headbutts and attacks using his palms. Dhalsim keeps opponents at a distance with his stretching limbs, calmly meditating while frustrating them. The boxer only uses his fists and corners opponents like in real boxing. But, designing this level of uniqueness for all 12 characters takes an enormous amount of time. As Noritaka Funamizu recalls, "We spent hours and hours studying all the possible moves from the different fighting styles of each character, then tried to make them more spectacular by exaggerating them as much as possible. For some characters like Blanka, it was even harder since they weren't directly based on real martial arts. We kept changing things over and over. We were going crazier and crazier. It felt like we were going in circles and would never finish. The work on the characters took an unbelievable amount of time." Meanwhile, Akira Nishitani keeps repeating the same thing. The most important aspect is animation. The characters need to feel alive, which means drawing every single frame of every animation for every move by hand. It's absurd. That's why there are 20 designers on the project and even with that many people, with moves constantly being reworked, development turns into a nightmare. The project drags on and on. While all this is happening, Akiman surprises everyone. He approaches his superiors and in a dead silent room announces that he wants to reuse the same control system as the early versions of Street Fighter, the pressure-sensitive punch buttons that had quickly been abandoned. "I really wanted to use those pressure buttons, but no one would let me. I kept insisting because they made fights more engaging, but in the end, the cabinet would have been too expensive and difficult to maintain, so it was rejected. But I also found joystick controls difficult, especially for performing special moves with those circular motions. So I came up with two new ways to execute moves, tapping and charging." Akiman has just invented something fundamental, new ways to control characters. Some fighters like Guile or Honda won't rely solely on precise joystick motions like quarter circles, but instead on holding a direction for a short time before releasing it in the opposite direction. These are what we now call charge characters. He also introduces moves that are triggered simply by pressing a button very rapidly, known as tapping. What's particularly clever is how all of this ties into each character's identity. Motion-based characters, those requiring precise joystick inputs, represent the discipline, rigor, and precision of martial arts, just like Takashi Nishiyama had envisioned in Street Fighter. This fits characters like Sagat or Ken perfectly. Charge characters, on the other hand, are more defensive, focused on preparation and space control, embodying patience and discipline, which suits Honda, the sumo wrestler, or Guile, the American soldier. And tapping-based characters, with their more rhythmic and instinctive gameplay, represent raw power and speed, perfectly fitting a wild beast like Blanka. But then, another twist of fate occurs, one that would change the history of video games. As Funamizu recalls, "While I was doing a bug check in the car bonus stage, something strange happened. After landing a hit, I quickly pressed the light punch button and realized you could chain two attacks together. That wasn't supposed to be possible, so it was a bug. Since I wasn't used to it, the timing was very hard to reproduce. I showed it to the others. We talked about it, and we thought it was incredible. We started testing combos, and they were extremely difficult to perform, but they added depth. So, we decided to keep them in the game, thinking very few players would actually be able to pull them off. We never imagined this decision would change everything." One of the greatest revolutions in fighting games had just happened by pure accident. What would later be known as combos. From that point on, the project evolves yet again. The team now has to make sure these combos don't break the game across all characters, which ends up creating dozens of bugs. Developers later describe it as the buggiest game in Capcom's history. Combos add an entirely new massive layer of work. Months go by. The project is now over a year behind schedule. And yet, ever since discovering combos, the developers spend entire nights just playing the game. As Akira Yasuda remembers with nostalgia, We spent night after night playing in two-player mode, sometimes going over 100 matches in a row. It was insane. Everyone would gather at the office and we'd hold tournaments. It was incredible. We just couldn't stop playing. It's the only time in my life I've seen something like that. We had fallen in love with our own game. Meanwhile, one person brings a whole new dimension to the game, a young composer named Yoko Shimomura. At the time, she's struggling with depression. She goes home crying every evening, unable to hold it together even on public transport. She feels incompetent, convinced she can't do her job properly. One night she decides, "Tomorrow, I'm going to quit." But by sheer coincidence, there's no composer available for Street Fighter II. So, the very next day, she's asked to work on the game's music. She can't really refuse. There's no one else to take her place. Reluctantly, she starts working. Then something shifts. As she looks at the game's visuals, its characters and stages, she suddenly feels inspired. She loves how unapologetically cliché everything is. It makes her laugh a lot. The light-hearted tone of the game begins to lift her spirits, and she starts to feel much better as the days pass by. So, she decides to lean into it, composing highly caricatural themes to match the tone of the game. Drawing from the different countries represented, she creates simple, catchy melodies, almost like national anthems, which fits perfectly with a game where fights take place all around the world. Her compositions often carry a heroic, slightly epic feel, supporting the intensity of the battles. For the first time, Shimomura feels proud of her work. She senses there's something special about these tracks, without realizing that decades later, they would be listened to tens of millions of times, and that she would perform them in concerts herself. On February 14th, 1991, Street Fighter II: The World Warrior is finally complete. After an intense final crunch period lasting several weeks, spent testing every possible combo across every character to eliminate as many bugs as possible. Akiman recalls developers breaking down in tears, collapsing from exhaustion at their desks after countless sleepless nights. Some later describe it as the adventure of a lifetime. They don't have much confidence in the game. They're convinced it won't succeed in Japan. They understand that the core appeal lies in fighting other players, but at the time, playing against strangers in arcades isn't really part of Japanese culture. So, they hope the game will succeed internationally, because otherwise, it could be a major failure for Capcom. In fact, Capcom is so unsure about the Japanese market that they make an unusual decision to release the game simultaneously in Japan and overseas. But just before release, Capcom USA reviews the game and starts to panic. The boxer character is named Mike Bison, clearly inspired by Mike Tyson, which could lead to serious legal trouble. In a rush, they swap character names to obscure the reference. In the international version, Mike Bison becomes Balrog. Balrog becomes Vega, and Vega becomes Bison, creating decades of confusion. After 2 and 1/2 years of development, an eternity at the time, Street Fighter II is finally released in arcades. A game that, in many ways, should never have existed. Everything about its creation feels almost miraculous. Street Fighter was created by someone who originally wanted to become a journalist but couldn't find work. The six-button control scheme was invented as a replacement for abandoned pressure-sensitive buttons. The war between Capcom and SNK led to Nishiyama's departure, which brought Akiman and Nishitani onto the project. Final Fight came out of a component shortage and allowed Capcom to invest far more money into the sequel. The combo system came from a bug. The music was composed by someone who was on the verge of quitting the industry altogether. All of these unlikely circumstances came together to create a truly unique game. A game that players would finally discover on March 7th, 1991. The development team rushes into Japanese arcades hoping to see players gathered around the machines, but their doubts quickly prove justified. The launch of Street Fighter II in Japan is far from drawing crowds. Most cabinets are empty or have just a single player. It's the same story in the United States. Early market tests are underwhelming and Capcom starts to worry. There's nothing surprising about it. At the time the Super Nintendo Entertainment System has just launched in Japan to massive success. Outside Japan, Super Mario Brothers III is breaking every sales record. Arcade gaming is widely seen as a dying market with revenue steadily declining as home consoles take over. Some game magazines have even dropped their arcade sections entirely. The first real spark of success for Street Fighter II comes from the United Kingdom and France. It sounds almost unreal, but French magazine Tilt >> [music] >> reports that the original Street Fighter was such a major success in France that it was still present in arcades 3 years after release. The one-on-one fighting culture had taken hold particularly well there. So, when Street Fighter II arrives, it's an immediate hit. The same thing happens in the UK and in Spain. Western Europe embraces the game right away, even though the arcade market there is smaller than elsewhere. Then, in the United States, about a month after release, word of mouth begins to spread. Suddenly, Street Fighter II explodes in popularity, catching even Capcom off guard. Arcades are flooded with players spending hours dueling complete strangers. As Capcom ramps up supply to meet demand, [music] things escalate even further. Lines start forming in front of the machines, which is rare, and week after week, the phenomenon grows. It's remarkable. Street Fighter II brings American players back into arcades at the exact moment the Super Nintendo is launching in the country. GamePro reports arcades with as many as eight Street Fighter II machines in a single location, whereas most games usually have just one or two. In Japan, Capcom actively encourages players to fight each other. Even though playing against strangers isn't part of the culture at the time, they push for it, and gradually, it takes off. Within a month, the game goes from not even being in the top 10 to becoming the number one arcade game in Japan. It ends up reshaping social habits, as thousands of players start competing against strangers every day, forming strong bonds through the game. Very quickly, Street Fighter II machines start appearing everywhere: cafes, bars, hair salons, pizzerias, restaurants, hotel lobbies, airports, bookstores, even bakeries. Some players even buy their own arcade cabinets to have the game at home. Japanese press reports that some employees quit their jobs to open arcades dedicated entirely to Street Fighter II. The phenomenon is unprecedented. At a time when arcade gaming is supposedly dying, Street Fighter II takes over the world in a way no arcade game ever [music] had before. David Snook, founder of the highly respected Coin Slot Magazine and an arcade publisher himself, writes, "I've been in the arcade business for 25 years and I've never seen a game with an impact as great as Street Fighter II. It even surpasses Space Invaders." John Sturgides from Electrocoin confirms, "We've been distributing arcade machines since 1976 >> [music] >> and this is without a doubt the most popular arcade game of all time. We've already delivered eight cabinets to some arcades. Usually, they take one per game, maybe two if it's exceptional. Here, they're taking eight." Capcom organizes official tournaments. In Japan, the All Japan Street II Championship is initially planned for 128 players, but due to overwhelming demand, it has been expanded to 512 participants selected by lottery. The demand is so intense that tournaments are held almost every month across multiple Japanese cities, drawing thousands of spectators, something unheard of in 1991. In the United States, it's the same story. Multiple official tournaments are organized across different states, further boosting the game's visibility. The success is so massive that magazines are forced to dedicate space to arcade games again and journalists describe the game using the most extreme superlatives. If I told you that I attempted a challenge
where I had to use six legendary Pokémon on the same team, you'd probably wonder what the big deal is. Legendary Pokémon are supposed to be
the strongest Pokémon in the game after all. But if I told you that the only legendary Pokémon that I was allowed to use were the six Regi Pokémon, you'd probably get really concerned really quickly. That's because not only were the Regis
clearly not designed to be used together, they mostly weren't designed to be used at all. Let me explain. Half of the Regis were introduced back in Generation 3, and I think that they are excellent Pokemon for what they're designed to be. Three mysterious golems that you could only encounter by solving puzzles, unlike anything that most players would have experienced before. Their designs are ancient and bizarre, and that's only amplified by their complex unlock requirements. I think they add a ton to the worldbuilding of the 3rd Generation. However, back in Generation 3, competitive play was clearly not in the forefront of the developer's minds. And so each of the original three Regis has a number of, we'll call them limitations. Each Regi is the sole avatar of a single type, either Rock, Steel, or Ice. This isn't inherently a problem, but unfortunately the types of two of the original three Regis are terrible. Rock and Ice are types that can be strong, but they aren't here. And that's because the Regis have stats that entirely gear them towards defense. Regirock and Regice have absurd physical and special bulk, respectively, some of the highest in the game, in fact; and Registeel meets in the middle to be bulky on both sides. Being bulky is usually quite good, but here the problem is that these original Regis can't actually do anything with their bulk. They have offensive stats below par by Generation 3 standards. Comparing them to the monsters we have in Generation 9 is just completely unfair. Actually, the offensive stats might be salvageable if not for the speed stats. Each of the Regis is 50 base speed, naturally 10 points lower than a Pokemon like Incineroar, a Pokemon known for being slow. Attacking after your opponent is a huge problem in competitive play, and the original three Regis really have no way to get around this. Fortunately for me, they introduced three more of these ancient paperweights later that could fix the problems of the original trio. The next Regi to be introduced is Regigigas, a Pokemon that's basically the butt of an inside joke at Game Freak. While at first glance it looks absurdly strong due to its ridiculously good stats, that's before we consider its ability Slow Start. This ability cuts its attack and speed stats in half for five whole turns, and this timer is reset if Regigigas ever switches out. It is true that Regigigas has seen competitive play in the past, but it's almost entirely when it's been paired with Weezing, a Pokemon who shuts down all abilities while it's on the field. I'm not allowed to use Weezing here, and none of the other Regis get any way to remove Slow Start. The astute among you will have noticed a problem that I haven't even had time to bring up yet, which is that of the four Pokemon on our team so far, all of them share a weakness to fighting type moves. Add together that they all have 70 speed stat or lower and can't do any meaningful damage, and you can see why I said that these Pokemon clearly weren't designed to be used together. Fortunately, there are two Regis left that can save us, and to their credit, they don't share the same weaknesses as the other four. Instead, they have new ones. These two Regis were added in the DLC of Sword and Shield, four generations after Regigigas, and it's pretty clear that they were actually designed with competitive play in mind. Each of them could do something quite well. Regieleki is the single fastest Pokemon ever made, and Regidrago is actually the only Regi to have consistent success in Scarlet and Violet due to its role as an offensive powerhouse. While these two are overall a lot stronger than their predecessors, these two Regis have a new weakness. They don't learn any coverage moves. Regieleki is especially guilty of this back in Sword and Shield, when Regieleki was a strong Dynamax attacker. Players would run Bounce and Hyper Beam as its best non-Electric moves. For reference, I have a story about how bad one of these moves is, and the other has not seen any competitive play outside of this moment. Regidrago is slightly better due to it learning one useful coverage move, but that's it. If you want these Pokemon to do well, you better hope that Electric and Dragon moves will do the trick. You may notice that neither of these two Regis resist Fighting, although at least they aren't weak to it. You may also notice that Regieleki adds a weakness to Ground, meaning that half of our team is weak to Ground type attacks. Regidrago is the only Pokemon with a Water resistance. Registeel is our only Fairy or Dragon resistance. Honestly, the whole type chart is a mess. When I decided that I wanted to attempt this challenge, I spotted these weaknesses pretty quickly. What I failed to realize, however, is that the six Regis have another, even more problematic weakness involving their moves. It's true that Regieleki, and Regidrago lack useful coverage moves, but all of the Regis lack the useful supporting moves that tie most competitive teams together, moves like Fake Out, Rage Powder and Follow Me, help your defensive Pokemon to protect your crucial and more frail offensive pieces. Moves like Tailwind and Trick Room help your Pokemon move before your foes. Priority moves like Aqua Jet, Sucker Punch, and Grassy Glide give another way for slower Pokemon to move before fast ones, and moves like Pollen Puff and Life Dew can help keep your bulky Pokemon alive. But as it turns out, not one of the Regis learns a single one of these moves, so not only is this challenge already extremely difficult due to the myriad of weaknesses that these Pokemon share, but I also lack almost every tool commonly used to mitigate said weaknesses. Unlike many of my other challenge runs where I'm limited to one type of Pokemon or using only Unevolved Pokemon, here I only have these six Pokemon to choose from. I can't just use Masquerain, or Tauros, or Litten if I want Intimidate on the team. Oh wait, yeah, I haven't even mentioned that none of the Regis get useful abilities when it comes to, you know, supporting the team. In the middle of bustling trains, crowded crossings, and packed city streets, many people in Japan are quietly struggling with loneliness. The country has the oldest population in the world with nearly 30% of people aged 65 or older. And as younger generations move away from work and families become smaller, more elderly Japanese are being left to live and die alone. [music] Government figures show nearly 77,000 people were found dead alone in their homes last year. Some undiscovered for days, even months. It's known in Japanese as kodokushi, or lonely death. Toyama Heights in Tokyo is one of Japan's oldest public housing estates, where several such cases have been reported. 88-year-old Setsuko Kurahara has been living alone here for more than 15 years. Her children are grown up and live far away. She spends most days with other elderly residents who live in the estate. >> I have some incurable diseases and I'm constantly ill. I have to go to the hospital a lot. If I can't walk anymore, I'll go to a nursing home. But right now, I'll stay here with everyone. >> One unlikely support network is a company that delivers yogurt every day. For some seniors, the brief doorstep conversation may be the only human contact they have all day. >> They really look forward to us coming and they wait patiently for us in the morning. If they seem unwell, we don't want them to collapse, so we report it to the company immediately in those situations. >> But loneliness isn't just a problem among the elderly. Experts say social isolation is also increasing among younger generations facing intense work pressure, shrinking social circles, and increasing emotional disconnection. It's become so widespread that Japan created a Minister of Loneliness position in 2021, tasked with combating isolation. Some people seek help through hotlines and mental health support applications. It's created an industry worth billions of dollars. >> One example of that is a range of online platforms where users can book a time with a conversation partner, someone to talk to either by video or phone, not for counseling, but simply for company. In a country where many people say they struggle to find regular social contact, it turns conversation itself into something that can be bought on demand. >> The loneliness crisis is already reshaping the way people live, work, and interact with each other. But without deeper social connections, isolation could become one of Japan's defining challenges in the years to come. >> Patrick Fok, Al Jazeera, Tokyo.

Safety first for this driver, strapping on his seatbelt as he settles into the driver's seat. One big problem. He's just seven years old. Dashboard cameras captured Daniel Wittenbach taking his family's SUV for a morning joy ride. He almost hit another work vehicle. It was a boy, a child. He's driving the wrong way into oncoming traffic in Middletown, Ohio. Cars swerve to avoid young Daniel, who's a decade shy of getting his license. You can hear him playing music along the way. But then the inevitable happens. He hits a median and slows down. Another driver tries to help, wrapping a shirt around his hands, trying to smash in the window. Only then does Daniel think he's going to get into trouble. Stop! (thump on window) It's my mom's car! He didn't understand that people were trying to help him and trying to stop the vehicle. Another driver tries to body block the SUV but it still hits this Jeep head on. Ow! You're okay, you're okay. Daniel wasn't hurt and remains blissfully unaware of the danger that he was in. So where was he going? His only response is, "Well I just wanted a Slushy from Speedway." Turns out his parents keep the keys stored high on a wall, but Daniel's a climber. I'm just thankful that he's alive. (outro music)

a fermented soybean, for breakfast in Japan. And since kids start eating it from
when they're babies, they learn to love it. It's customary in Japan to say

I told you to turn off YouTube. Oh, she's not supposed to watch YouTube during meals, but she couldn't help but tap that play button. Tomomi, does she often watch YouTube? All the time. ways to convince Karin to brush her teeth. Let's sleep! It usually takes her about
15 to 20 minutes to put Karin to bed. - Good night, Karin.
- Good night. Once asleep, Tomomi finally has a chance
